coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots from polystyrene for three analytes sensing and their logic recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haohao
Li, Yanrong
Xu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: The increasing plastic wastes such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and polystyrene (PS), are urgent environment problem threatening human health. The reuse and conversion of PS wastes remains a substantial challenge. Herein, a conversion of PS plastics to fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via a two-step method involving nitration and solvothermal treatment was proposed. These CDs exhibited sensitive fluorescence quenching to Hg2+ and Fe3+ due to the existence of amino and hydroxyl functional groups. The presence of GSH made the fluorescence of N-CDs/Hg2+ restore, but no apparent recovery can be found in the sample of N-CDs/Fe3+. A “three in one” sensing assay for Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH was therefore fabricated, and the sensing performance for Hg2+ sensing was demonstrated using river samples as mode. These selective recognitions and sensing were originated from electron transfer process. Benefiting from three analytes inputs and relevant fluorescence results, the logic gates of IMP and recognition between Hg2+ and Fe3+ were achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polystyrene
Multi-analytes
Sensing and recognition
Logic gates

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
Detection of Hg2+ in real sample
Fluorescence lifetime test of N-CDs samples
Stability of N-CDs in pH and saline solutions
Fluorescence sensor based on N-CDs for recognition and detection of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH
Sensitivity of the fluorescence sensor to Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH
The method of Hg2+ and Fe3+ recognition
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in aqueous solution (Inset: images of N-CDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (b) UV–vis spectra of N-CDs. (c) TEM image of N-CDs. (d) Particle size distribution of N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs. High resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s peaks.

(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with various metal ions (Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, K+ Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, Na+) at a concentration of 300 μM.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0 to 300 μM). (b) Linear response curve between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different concentrations of Fe3+ (from 0 to 300 μM). (d) Linear response curve between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs system at different concentrations of GSH (from 0 to 300 μM). (f) Linear response curve between fluorescence intensity and GSH concentration.

(a) IMP logic operation based on N-CDs at Hg2+ as input 1, and GSH as input 2. (b) Normalized FL intensity of the N-CDs-based IMP gates with different input combinations; The symbol and the truth table of the IMP gates. (c) Fluorescent probes respond to Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of fluorescence switching off and on of N-CDs under the action of metal ions and GSH.
Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples (river water) using N-CDs as sensing probe.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots from polystyrene for three analytes sensing and their logic recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Specific detection and recognition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by fluorescent probes based on carbon dots.

The increasing plastic wastes such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and polystyrene (PS), are urgent environment problem threatening human health. The reuse and conversion of PS wastes remains a substantial challenge. Herein, a conversion of PS plastics to fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via a two-step method involving nitration and solvothermal treatment was proposed. These CDs exhibited sensitive fluorescence quenching to Hg2+ and Fe3+ due to the existence of amino and hydroxyl functional groups. The presence of GSH made the fluorescence of N-CDs/Hg2+ restore, but no apparent recovery can be found in the sample of N-CDs/Fe3+. A “three in one” sensing assay for Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH was therefore fabricated, and the sensing performance for Hg2+ sensing was demonstrated using river samples as mode. These selective recognitions and sensing were originated from electron transfer process. Benefiting from three analytes inputs and relevant fluorescence results, the logic gates of IMP and recognition between Hg2+ and Fe3+ were achieved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastics are widely used in society due to their low cost and high durability [1], especially the COVID-19 pandemic intensifies the global demand for plastics [2]. It is non-negligible that the waste generated after the use of plastics has serious harm to the ecological environment and biological health [3,4]. Polystyrene (PS), as one of the most widely used plastics, is confronted with the difficulty of degradation and can exist in the environment for a long time [5]. With long-term weathering effects, these PS wastes were cut into tiny plastic residue and had harmful impact on natural resources involving water, soil and air, which were subsequently intake by animals and human being [6,7]. Nowadays, the traditional disposal methods for plastics are mainly focused on landfills and combustion, which produces toxic gases to pollute the air and cause climate change [8]. It is deeply desired to find a facile solution for the conversion of plastic waste to valuable materials. The synthesis of carbon materials may be a good target because plastics carbonization can be easily achieved [9,10].

Carbon dots (CDs) as an emerging promising carbon nanomaterial, are extensively applied in sensing [11], biological imaging [12,13], drug delivery [14], and anti-counterfeiting [15], owing to their excellent optical properties, low costs, and good biocompatibility. The use of plastic waste to synthesize CDs has been reported. Song et al. [16] synthesized solid-state fluorescent CDs in solvothermal method using PS as a precursor. The fluorescence emission was tuned with the volume of the solvent and applied to fabricate LED devices. Chaudhary et al. [10] synthesized three fluorescent CDs from polystyrene plastic wastes from three different sources. The fluorescence quenching of three CDs solutions in the presence of Cu2+ realized the detection of Cu2+. Chan et al. [17] used PET bottles as raw materials to synthesize nitrogen doped fluorescent CDs, and the prepared fluorescent sensors can detect Fe3+ and Cu2+. Kumari et al. [18] used polyolefin waste to synthesize fluorescent CDs via a hydrothermal process, and applied in the detection of Cu2+. Ramanan et al. [19] synthesized multifunctional fluorescent CDs by solvothermal method using PS waste. The CDs showed high selectivity and high sensitivity to Au3+, and can be used for Au3+ detection. Although some CDs prepared from plastics have been reported, most fluorescent sensors based on these CDs can be used to detect one kind of metal ions. Therefore, plastic-derived CDs for multi-target sensing are urgently required because multi-targets sensor can provide abundant information in single matrix.

In this work, we proposed a nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) using PS as precursor via a two-step process involving nitration and solvothermal reaction for three analytes sensing and logic gates construction. The fluorescence of N-CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ and Fe3+, demonstrating these N-CDs can be used as a sensor for Hg2+ and Fe3+. Different to previously works on sensing of metal ions, a logic distinguishing and sensing method was proposed with import of GSH based on an off–on process in N-CDs/Hg2+, but the fluorescence in N-CDs/Fe3+ was not retained. Therefore, the recognition between Fe3+ and Hg2+ was achieved. The linear ranges of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH were 0.5–20 μΜ, 0.25–10 μΜ, 1–50 μM and the minimum detection concentrations were performed at 0.5 μΜ, 0.25 μΜ and 1 μM, respectively. This method for Hg2+ detection was also performed in the real river samples, demonstrating its excellent sensing performance. Simple, green, and low-cost method for preparing N-CDs provides an effective way to PS plastic convertion, and “three in one” sensing performance highlights the promising applications in the detection of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH.

Polystyrene (PS) was purchased from Beijing Inokay Technology Co., ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Company Limited (Tianjin, China). Nitric acid (HNO3) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Glutathione and cysteine were purchased from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China), and other amino acids were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., ltd. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The morphologies of the synthesized N-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai 20) at an accelerating voltage of 200 KeV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were recorded using a Bruker Vector 22 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using a Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB250 Xi and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded using an Agilent Carry 100 UV–vis spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) excitation, emission spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FS920P). The ζ-potentials of N-CDs were tested at 25 °C recorded with a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, British).

500 mg of polystyrene was dissolved in concentrated nitric acid, the solution was transferred to the round-bottomed flask and reacted in an oil bath at 130 ℃ for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, it was naturally cooled to room temperature, diluted with a large amount of deionized water, and filtered until the filtrate was neutral. Finally, nitrated polystyrene was obtained.

Using nitrated polystyrene and ethylenediamine as precursors, N-CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method. Generally, 500 μL of ethylenediamine is first added to a 10 mL DMF solution containing 150 mg of nitrated polystyrene, sonicated for 15 min to completely dissolve and form a transparent solution. Then the mixed solution was sealed in a 30 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. The sealed autoclave was then kept in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the completion of the reaction, then the polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the solution was taken out to obtain N-CDs. The N-CDs solution was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane. The diluted solution shows blue fluorescence emission under ultraviolet light. N-CDs solution was dried at 60 °C to obtain a brown powder. The obtained N-CDs were re-dissolved in water for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 1.0 mL of 0.58 mg·mL-1N-CDs and different concentrations of Hg2+ or Fe3+ sample solutions were added to centrifuge tubes, and then the volume was made up to 2.0 mL with deionized water. Then, the mixtures were mixed well and kept 3 min. Fluorescence spectra with emission wavelengths in the range of 385–630 nm were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

Similar to the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+, 1.0 mL of 0.58 mg·mL-1N-CDs, 0.4 mL of 1 mM Hg2+ sample solution and GSH with different concentrations were added to centrifuge tubes, and then the volume was made up to 2.0 mL with deionized water. Then, the mixtures were mixed well and kept 3 min. Fluorescence spectra with emission wavelengths ranging from 385 to 630 nm were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of using N-CDs as fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ in real samples, the river water of Tianjin North Canal was selected, and the river water was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. 0.5 mL of N-CDs solution with a concentration of 0.58 mg·mL−1, 1.0 mL of river water and Hg2+ of different concentrations were added to a series of 2.0 mL centrifuge tubes, and then the volume was made up to 2.0 mL with deionized water. Then, the mixtures were mixed well and kept 3 min. Fluorescence spectra with emission wavelengths in the range of 385 to 630 nm were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Each sample was measured in parallel three times. The sensitivity of detecting Hg2+ in real samples based on N-CDs fluorescent probe was confirmed by standard addition recovery method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence lifetime test of N-CDs samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence lifetimes experiment was conducted with the fluorescence spectrometer. N-CDs sample is excited at 340 nm and emitted at 420 nm, which is obtained by nonlinear fitting of single exponential function It = I0 e-kt.

For the IMP operation, 0.58 mg·mL−1 of the starting solution of N-CDs was first added to four centrifuge tubes. Then add 0.2 mM Hg2+ defined as input-1 and 0.3 mM GSH defined as input-2. Logic operations are performed according to the four corresponding states (0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0) and (1, 1), respectively.

The optical properties of N-CDs were investigated in Fig. 1
a and b. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the optimal excitation wavelength of N-CDs is 340 nm and the optimal emission wavelength focus at 420 nm. The N-CDs aqueous solution was pale yellow under daylight and exhibited bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light excitation. Fig. 1 b represented the UV–vis absorption of the N-CDs. The UV–vis absorption peak at 215 nm was attributed to π–π* transition. As shown in Fig. 1 c, the morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs was spherical and mono-disperse. The size distribution of N-CDs was in range of 2.66–5.18 nm with an average size of 3.70 nm ( Fig. 1 d). These results indicated that fluorescent N-CDs were successfully prepared.

In FT-IR spectra (
Figure S1
), the NO bond asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration were observed at 1520 cm−1 and 1346 cm−1 in nitrated polystyrene, respectively. In the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, the broad band appearing at 3278 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH and N–H, and the strong peaks at 1540 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of the conjugated structure CC [16,19]. Three peaks centered at 1395, 1670, and 1261 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond [20], CO bond, and COC bond, respectively [21]. These results demonstrated that oxygen and nitrogen are retained in N-CDs. The XPS spectra also provided the functional group information of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2
a, N-CDs had peaks of C, N and O with relative element contents of 79.36, 12.41 and 8.23 at%, respectively. The detailed peak fitting of the C1s peak of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 2 b. The C1s peaks at 284.6, 285.8, and 287.8 eV were associated with CC/CC, CN/CO and CO, respectively [22,23]. Two N1s peaks were fitted at 399.3 and 399.7 eV, ascribed to pyridinic N and amino N, respectively ( Fig. 2 c) [24]. The O1s spectrum was fitted by two peaks at 531 and 531.6 eV, which were attributed to CO and CO, respectively ( Fig. 2 d) [22]. These results indicated that N-CDs contain abundant hydroxyl and amino groups, and have potential for metal ions sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of N-CDs in pH and saline solutions
Fluorescence sensor based on N-CDs for recognition and detection of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the N-CDs-based ion sensing system for Hg2+ and Fe3+, various metal ions were tested under the same conditions with a concentration of 300 μM. At the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence of N-CDs and mixed solutions of 16 kinds of metal ions involving Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, K+, Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, Na+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 , clear fluorescence quenching effects were observed in the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+. However, the mixtures between N-CDs and other metal ions had slight fluorescence change. These results indicated that N-CDs were highly selective to Hg2+ and Fe3+. Considering the harmfulness of Hg2+ and Fe3+, it was valuable to fabricate sensor using the N-CDs for detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the fluorescence sensor to Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantitative detections on Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH were performed to evaluate the sensitivity of N-CDs. For the Hg2+ fluorescence quenching experiments on N-CDs (Fig. 4
a), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 420 nm gradually decreased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 300 μM. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the Hg2+ concentration was in the range of 0.5–20 μM, and the linear equation was I/I0 = -0.017x + 0.991 (x is the Hg2+ concentration, I0 is the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs alone, I is the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of N-CDs and Hg2+, R2 = 0.996). The minimum detection concentration of Hg2+ was 0.5 μM. The Fe3+ fluorescence quenching results were similar to N-CDs/Hg2+ and in accord with turn off mode ( Fig. 4 c). As shown in Fig. 4 d, the Fe3+ concentration was in the range of 0.25–10 μM, and the linear equation was I/I0 = -0.00608x + 0.949 (x is the Fe3+ concentration, I0 is the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs alone, I is the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of N-CDs and Fe3+, R2 = 0.989). The minimum detection concentration of Fe3+ is 0.25 μM. The above results showed that the N-CDs were suitable as a fluorescence probe for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection.

Furthermore, the N-CDs/Hg2+ system were added with different concentrations of GSH to study the fluorescence off–on process. As shown in Fig. 4 e, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs/Hg2+ system at 420 nm was gradually enhanced with increasing GSH concentration, revealing a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the GSH concentration with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.993 ( Fig. 4 f). The linear equation was I/I0 = 0.00168x + 0.352 (x is the GSH concentration, I represents the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/Hg2+ system added with different concentrations of GSH, I0 represents the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/Hg2+ system). The signal could be obtained as low as 1 μM of concentration of GSH. The above results showed that GSH can restore the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs/Hg2+ system, and the fluorescent probe based on N-CDs/Hg2+ system is sensitive to GSH via a fluorescence off–on process.

Notably, these N-CDs not only applied for two target metal ions detection, but also responded to GSH, which could be called as multi-target sensor [25,26] and provide abundant information in single matrix.

The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be triggered by Hg2+ and GSH. Therefore, Hg2+ and GSH can be used as chemical inputs in the logic operation based on the fluorescence response of N-CDs. The relative fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 420 nm was set to 0.5 as the fluorescence threshold. If the fluorescence intensity of sensor was higher than 0.5, the result was defined as output 1. If the intensity was lower than 0.5, the result was defined as output 0.

IMPLICATION (IMP) is a binary logic operation which produces the output of 0 only if the input 1 is present and the input 2 is absent. N-CDs showed blue fluorescence (FL output = 1) in the original state (input = 0, 0) in the absence of Hg2+ and GSH. Hg2+ (input = 1, 0) can trigger the FL quenching of N-CDs (FL output = 0). In the presence of both Hg2+ and GSH (input = 1, 1), the solution returns to its original state, thus showing blue fluorescence (FL output = 1). As GSH had slight quenching effect on the N-CDs (input = 0, 1), the solution still maintained blue fluorescence (FL output = 1). As shown in Fig. 5
a, the result of the N-CDs FL response was closely related to the execution of the IMP logical operation ( Fig. 5 b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The method of Hg2+ and Fe3+ recognition
ce:section.ce:para: When fluorescent probes detect multi-target metal ions, how to distinguish targets was still a challenge. These N-CDs not only builds logic gates, but also provides a method for distinguishing Hg2+ and Fe3+. Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH were used as chemical inputs respectively. The presence of chemical input was defined as 1, while the absence of chemical input was defined as 0. The chemical output corresponds to the fluorescence response. The output was 0 defined as the fluorescence off, the output was 1 defined as the fluorescence on ( Fig. 5 c). Results of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH response to fluorescent probes were exhibited in Fig. 5 c. In the presence of only Hg2+ (Input = 1, 0, 0) or Fe3+ (Input = 0, 1, 0), N-CDs fluorescence was quenched (Output = 0). In the presence of Hg2+ and GSH (Input = 1, 0, 1), the fluorescence of N-CDs recovered (Output = 1). In the presence of Fe3+ and GSH (Input = 0, 1, 1), the N-CDs fluorescence was still quenched (Output = 0). Thus, the recognition between Hg2+ and Fe3+ can be achieved by adding GSH to the N-CDs system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, a kind of plastic-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was constructed in a green, simple, and low-cost method using polystyrene (PS) as carbon source. The good stability of the N-CDs was performed in various pH and NaCl solutions. Furthermore, sensitive sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+ was built. Notably, the recognition between Hg2+ and Fe3+ was achieved through the addition of GSH. After adding GSH, the fluorescence of N-CDs/Hg2+ system was restored, whereas N-CDs/Fe3+ system showed almost no fluorescence enhancement. This multi-analytes assay exhibited some particular advantages. (I) the N-CDs were prepared from PS, which expand an effective route to deal with PS plastics. (II) “Three in one” sensor involving sensitive sensing of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH was achieved. (III) A “IMP” logic gates was constructed, and recognition between Hg2+ and Fe3+ was performed in detail. Therefore, these plastic-derived N-CDs showed great potential in environmental analysis, and suitable for the detection and quantification of Hg2+, Fe3+ and GSH.
Haohao Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Yanrong Li: Data curation. Yang Xu: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21605036), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2021202056) are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.110369.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
